content.body: ECMA-426 normatively references ECMA-262 machinery: https://tc39.es/ecma426/#sec-MatchSourceMapURL

content.body: why? i want it very much
if it's not nuts to put it all in the same app, then it shouldn't be nuts to put it in the same doc :-p
where would you even establish the cutoff? Does it include HTTP? WebSocket? DNS? QUIC?
everything. i want a single document that defines everything a web browser needs to be compliant.
iow i don't agree with "you have to draw a line somewhere" - you can just not draw a line
clarified above, I was specifically referring to other specs referencing ECMA-262 regular expressions
as was i
oh, I didn't see any reference to regular expressions in ECMA-424
in that case, good luck to you
oh 427 is what i meant
too many numbers

content.body: I'm working on the discussed updates for the typedarray find within proposal and I'm stuck on figuring out alternative names as discussed... the request was that we find a name that clearly represents that we're looking for a subsequence... other than the Too-On-The-Nose `findSubsequence` which works but makes me cry a little, I'd love folks to suggest some alternatives colors for the bike shed.
content.formatted_body: I'm working on the discussed updates for the typedarray find within proposal and I'm stuck on figuring out alternative names as discussed... the request was that we find a name that clearly represents that we're looking for a subsequence... other than the Too-On-The-Nose findSubsequence which works but makes me cry a little, I'd love folks to suggest some alternatives colors for the bike shed.

content.body: surely `indexOfSequence`, `find` means something else
I'm rather hoping that import-text will be a counterpoint, an example of how a proposal can get implemented across multiple specifications in a record short time, rather than nearly a decade...
`findX` in most of the core spec tends to mean "look for the element via a predicate callback function"
content.formatted_body: findX in most of the core spec tends to mean "look for the element via a predicate callback function"

content.body: I actually don't mind `Sequence` in the function name
content.formatted_body: I actually don't mind Sequence in the function name

content.body: honestly, `indexOfSequence` would be ok by me. it's clear, at least.
content.formatted_body: honestly, indexOfSequence would be ok by me. it's clear, at least.

content.body: ("subsequence" is overkill tho)
I think `indexOfSequence` is fine, though on the surface it leads me to believe that iterables and arrays might be an option.
content.formatted_body: I think indexOfSequence is fine, though on the surface it leads me to believe that iterables and arrays might be an option.

content.body: Or `Chunk` which is shorter
(part of my motivation is that I am still kind of hoping they can be an option later)
`indexOfChunk`, `includesChunk`
That's not a huge issue. I'd say `indexOfSubarray`, except `.subarray()` has a specific meaning that might be at odds
content.formatted_body: That's not a huge issue. I'd say indexOfSubarray, except .subarray() has a specific meaning that might be at odds

content.body: `indexOfSequence` WFM
ok, that's multiple votes for `indexOfSequence` ... works for me
content.formatted_body: ok, that's multiple votes for indexOfSequence ... works for me

content.body: thanks all

... you know... we could _technically_ overload `indexOf`. it does strict equality for the needle (no coercion) so `TA.indexOf(otherTA)` will always return `false`, and it's possible no one is relying on that
content.formatted_body: ... you know... we could technically overload indexOf. it does strict equality for the needle (no coercion) so TA.indexOf(otherTA) will always return false, and it's possible no one is relying on that

content.body: but, new method is probably better, I guess
While I think supporting arbitrary iterables is perhaps overkill, it's unfortunate that `u8.indexOfSequence([0, 1, 2])` would be disallowed for convenience.
content.formatted_body: While I think supporting arbitrary iterables is perhaps overkill, it's unfortunate that u8.indexOfSequence([0, 1, 2]) would be disallowed for convenience.
